question_text: Urea fertiliser is the most widely used straight nitrogenous fertiliser in India and contains approximately what percentage of nitrogen?
explanation: Urea contains approximately 46 percent nitrogen, making it the highest-analysis solid nitrogen fertiliser commercially available. It is in the amide form and must be hydrolysed by the soil enzyme urease to ammonium before crops can use it. India mandates neem coating of all urea since 2015 to slow down hydrolysis and reduce nitrogen losses.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: About 20 percent nitrogen content
About 33 percent nitrogen content
About 46 percent nitrogen content
About 60 percent nitrogen content

elimination_hints.hint: 20 percent N is ammonium sulphate, not urea
33 percent N is ammonium nitrate, not urea
No common solid N fertiliser has 60 percent N

question_text: DAP (Di-ammonium Phosphate) is the most popular complex phosphatic fertiliser in India with which specific nutrient grade?
explanation: DAP has the grade 18-46-0, meaning it contains 18 percent nitrogen and 46 percent phosphorus pentoxide (P₂O₅) with no potassium. It is the single most consumed phosphatic fertiliser in India. It is an excellent starter fertiliser because both nitrogen and phosphorus placed together near the seed promote early root development.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: Grade 46-0-0 with nitrogen only
Grade 18-46-0 with nitrogen and phosphorus
Grade 0-0-60 with potassium only
Grade 10-26-26 with all three nutrients

elimination_hints.hint: 46-0-0 is urea, not DAP
0-0-60 is muriate of potash, not DAP
10-26-26 is an NPK complex, not DAP

question_text: Rhizobium biofertiliser is specifically used as a seed inoculant for which particular group of crops to enhance nitrogen nutrition?
explanation: Rhizobium is a symbiotic nitrogen-fixing bacterium that forms nodules on the roots of leguminous crops like chickpea, soybean, groundnut, pigeonpea and lentil. It converts atmospheric N₂ into ammonium, which the host plant uses for protein synthesis. Rhizobium inoculant is applied as seed coating before sowing legumes to ensure effective nodulation.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: Cereal crops like rice, wheat and maize only
Oilseed crops like mustard and sunflower only
Leguminous crops like chickpea, soybean and groundnut
Fibre crops like cotton and jute exclusively

elimination_hints.hint: Cereals do not form nodules with Rhizobium
Non-legume oilseeds do not nodulate with Rhizobium
Cotton and jute do not form root nodules with Rhizobium

question_text: Soil bulk density is most correctly defined as which specific physical measurement of a soil in the field?
explanation: Soil bulk density is defined as the mass of oven-dry soil per unit total volume (including pores), expressed as grams per cubic centimetre or megagrams per cubic metre. A typical mineral agricultural soil has bulk density of 1.1 to 1.6 g/cm³. Lower bulk density means more pore space, better aeration and easier root penetration.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: Mass of water held in soil per unit dry soil weight
Mass of oven-dry soil per unit total volume including pores
Total exchangeable cations per unit weight of soil
Number of soil organisms per gram of fresh field soil

elimination_hints.hint: Water mass per dry soil is moisture content, not bulk density
Exchangeable cations per weight is CEC, not bulk density
Organisms per gram is microbial count, not bulk density

question_text: Field capacity of a soil is defined as the soil water content at which specific hydrological condition after thorough wetting?
explanation: Field capacity is the amount of water retained in the soil after thorough wetting followed by free drainage until the downward movement of water essentially stops, usually within 24-48 hours. At field capacity, the large pores (macropores) have drained and are filled with air, while smaller pores still hold water by capillary forces available to plant roots.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: Water content when soil is completely saturated and waterlogged
Water content when plants are permanently wilting in the field
Water content after thorough wetting and free drainage has stopped
Water content when soil is completely air-dry at room temperature

elimination_hints.hint: Complete saturation is above field capacity, not at field capacity
Permanent wilting point is a different, lower water content
Air-dry soil is far below field capacity

question_text: The Permanent Wilting Point (PWP) of a soil is most correctly defined as the soil water content at which specific plant condition?
explanation: Permanent Wilting Point is the soil water content at which plants can no longer extract water fast enough to recover turgidity, even when placed in a saturated atmosphere, and wilt permanently. It corresponds to a soil water potential of about minus 15 bars (minus 1500 kPa). Available water for plants lies between field capacity and permanent wilting point.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: Water content when plants temporarily wilt at noon but recover at night
Water content at which plants wilt permanently and cannot recover turgidity
Water content when soil is fully saturated after heavy rain
Water content when soil is at field capacity after drainage

elimination_hints.hint: Temporary noon wilting is before PWP is reached
Full saturation is far above PWP
Field capacity is the upper limit of available water, not PWP

question_text: Integrated Nutrient Management (INM) in crop production is best described by which specific balanced approach to soil fertility?
explanation: INM is the judicious combined use of chemical fertilisers, organic manures (FYM, compost, vermicompost), biofertilisers (Rhizobium, Azotobacter, PSB), green manures and crop residues to maintain soil health and achieve sustained crop productivity. INM aims to reduce sole dependence on chemical fertilisers while maintaining yield and improving soil organic carbon.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: Using only chemical fertilisers in very high doses for maximum yield
Using only organic manures and completely banning chemical fertilisers
Combined use of chemical, organic, biofertilisers and green manures
Completely stopping all external nutrient inputs to the field

elimination_hints.hint: INM reduces sole chemical dependence, not maximises it
Complete ban on chemicals is organic farming, not INM
Stopping all inputs leads to nutrient depletion

question_text: Sodic or alkali soils in India are characterised by which specific chemical property that makes them problematic for crops?
explanation: Sodic (alkali) soils have high exchangeable sodium percentage (ESP above 15) and a pH typically above 8.5 to 10.0. Excess sodium on the exchange complex disperses clay, destroys soil structure and makes the soil nearly impervious to water and air. The standard reclamation amendment is gypsum, which supplies calcium to displace sodium from the exchange complex.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: Very low pH of 3.5 with extremely high iron content
High exchangeable sodium (ESP above 15) and pH above 8.5
Very high organic carbon of 10 percent like peat soils
Extremely sandy texture with zero clay content throughout

elimination_hints.hint: Very low pH is acid soil, not sodic soil
High organic carbon is peaty soil, not sodic
Sandy texture is not sodicity

question_text: A farmer needs to apply 100 kg nitrogen per hectare using urea (46% N). How many kilograms of urea per hectare are required?
explanation: If urea contains 46 percent nitrogen, then to apply 100 kg N per hectare the farmer needs 100 divided by 0.46 equals approximately 217 kg urea per hectare. This is a standard agronomic calculation: quantity of fertiliser equals nutrient required divided by nutrient content fraction. This conversion is essential for fertiliser purchase and application planning.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: About 46 kg of urea per hectare
About 100 kg of urea per hectare
About 217 kg of urea per hectare
About 460 kg of urea per hectare

elimination_hints.hint: 46 kg urea would give only about 21 kg N
100 kg urea gives only 46 kg N, not 100 kg
460 kg urea would provide 212 kg N, far too much

question_text: Gypsum (calcium sulphate dihydrate) is applied as a reclamation amendment specifically for which problematic soil type in India?
explanation: Gypsum is the standard amendment for reclaiming sodic or alkali soils. When gypsum is applied to sodic soil, the calcium from gypsum replaces sodium on the clay exchange complex, and the displaced sodium is leached out of the root zone by irrigation. This restores soil structure, permeability and aeration, making the soil productive again for rice-wheat or other crops.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: Sodic or alkali soils with high exchangeable sodium
Strongly acidic soils with very low pH below 4.5
Normal productive loam soils with neutral pH around 7
Peaty organic soils with very high organic matter content

elimination_hints.hint: Acidic soils need lime, not gypsum
Normal soils do not need gypsum reclamation
Peaty soils need drainage, not gypsum

question_text: Match the biofertiliser organisms in Column A with the specific nutrient contribution or crop association in Column B.
explanation: Rhizobium fixes nitrogen symbiotically in legume root nodules; Azotobacter fixes nitrogen freely (non-symbiotically) in the rhizosphere of many crops; Azospirillum is an associative N-fixer in cereal roots; and Phosphate Solubilising Bacteria (PSB) convert insoluble soil phosphates into soluble available phosphorus for plant uptake.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

column_b.text: Solubilises insoluble soil phosphates for plant use
Associative N-fixer in cereal roots like rice and wheat
Free-living N-fixer in rhizosphere of many crops
Symbiotic N-fixer in legume root nodules

elimination_hints.hint: Rhizobium is symbiotic, not a P-solubiliser
Rhizobium is not an associative cereal fixer
Rhizobium is not a free-living fixer

question_text: Match the following fertilisers in Column A with their correct nutrient grades listed in Column B as used in India.
explanation: Urea contains 46-0-0 (46% N only); DAP contains 18-46-0 (N and P); MOP contains 0-0-60 (60% K₂O only); and SSP contains 0-16-0 plus about 11 percent sulphur. Knowing the grade is essential for calculating the quantity of each fertiliser needed to fulfil a crop's recommended NPK dose.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: Urea is 46-0-0, not SSP grade
Urea is not potash 0-0-60
Urea is not DAP grade 18-46-0

question_text: Match the soil water constants in Column A with their correct definitions or practical descriptions in Column B.
explanation: Saturation is when all pores are filled with water; field capacity is water retained after free drainage stops (about minus 33 kPa); permanent wilting point is the water content at which plants wilt permanently (about minus 1500 kPa); and available water is the difference between field capacity and PWP, the actual water that crops can use.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

column_b.text: Difference between field capacity and permanent wilting point
Water content at which plants wilt permanently (minus 1500 kPa)
Water retained after free drainage stops (minus 33 kPa)
All soil pores are completely filled with water

elimination_hints.hint: Saturation is not the difference between FC and PWP
Saturation is not at minus 33 kPa
Saturation is not wilting point

question_text: Read the assertion and reason about neem-coated urea in India carefully and choose the most correct option below.
explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Neem oil coating of urea acts as a nitrification inhibitor, slowing down the conversion of ammonium to nitrate in the soil. This reduces losses through leaching of nitrate and denitrification to gaseous nitrogen oxides, thereby keeping more nitrogen in the root zone for a longer period and improving nitrogen use efficiency.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture
assertion: India mandates neem coating of all urea to improve nitrogen use efficiency in crops.
reason: Neem oil inhibits nitrification, slowing conversion of ammonium to nitrate and reducing nitrogen losses.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Slowing nitrification is the very mechanism that improves NUE
Nitrification inhibition by neem is well established
Neem-coated urea mandate is Indian government policy since 2015

question_text: Read the assertion and reason about gypsum application in sodic soils carefully and choose the most appropriate option below.
explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. The calcium ions from gypsum displace sodium ions from the clay exchange complex through mass action, and the displaced sodium is leached out of the root zone by irrigation. This restores soil aggregation, permeability and tilth, allowing roots to grow normally and crops to produce well.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture
assertion: Gypsum application followed by leaching reclaims sodic soils by restoring soil structure and permeability.
reason: Calcium from gypsum displaces sodium from the clay exchange complex and sodium is leached away.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Ca-Na exchange is the very mechanism of sodic reclamation
Ca displacing Na from clay is well established
Gypsum reclamation of sodic soils is well documented

question_text: Read the assertion and reason about soil organic carbon and fertility carefully and choose the option which best fits.
explanation: The assertion is true but the reason is false. Soil organic carbon improves fertility by increasing CEC, nutrient retention, water-holding capacity, microbial activity and soil aggregation. It does not reduce CEC — on the contrary, humus has a very high CEC (150-300 cmol/kg), much higher than most clay minerals, and is thus a major contributor to soil nutrient retention.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture
assertion: Higher soil organic carbon content generally improves soil fertility and crop productivity.
reason: Organic carbon reduces cation exchange capacity and nutrient retention of the soil drastically.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Organic carbon increases, not reduces CEC
The reason itself is chemically false
Higher SOC improving fertility is well established

question_text: Arrange the following steps of reclaiming sodic (alkali) soil in a farmer's field in their correct chronological order.
explanation: Reclaiming sodic soil starts with a soil test to determine Gypsum Requirement (GR). Gypsum is then broadcast over the field and mixed into the top soil. The field is irrigated heavily to dissolve the gypsum and leach out the displaced sodium from the root zone. Finally, rice (a tolerant crop) is grown as the first crop and over successive seasons soil structure improves for normal cropping.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

items.text: Grow rice as first tolerant crop on reclaimed soil
Conduct soil test to determine gypsum requirement
Broadcast gypsum and mix into topsoil of the field
Heavy irrigation to dissolve gypsum and leach sodium out

options.text: Q → R → S → P
P → Q → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → Q → S

elimination_hints.hint: Cropping cannot precede soil testing
Irrigation cannot precede gypsum application
Gypsum cannot be applied before testing requirement

question_text: Arrange the following nitrogen transformations in soil in the correct sequential order starting from urea application to the field.
explanation: When urea is applied to soil, it is first hydrolysed by the enzyme urease to ammonium carbonate, yielding ammonium ions. Ammonium is then oxidised by Nitrosomonas bacteria to nitrite, which is further oxidised by Nitrobacter to nitrate. This two-step process is called nitrification. Nitrate is the form most readily taken up by crop roots but is also susceptible to leaching.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

items.text: Nitrite oxidised to nitrate by Nitrobacter bacteria
Urea hydrolysed by urease enzyme to ammonium
Ammonium oxidised to nitrite by Nitrosomonas bacteria
Nitrate taken up by crop roots or lost by leaching

options.text: Q → R → P → S
S → P → R → Q
P → Q → S → R
R → S → Q → P

elimination_hints.hint: Crop uptake cannot precede urea hydrolysis
Nitrite formation cannot precede urea hydrolysis
Ammonium oxidation cannot precede urea hydrolysis

question_text: State whether the following statement about vermicompost as an organic manure in crop production is TRUE or FALSE with justification.
explanation: The statement is TRUE. Vermicompost is prepared by using earthworms (commonly Eisenia fetida or Eudrilus eugeniae) to decompose organic waste like cow dung, crop residues and kitchen waste. The earthworm castings are rich in available NPK, humic substances, beneficial microbes and growth hormones, making vermicompost a high-quality organic manure for INM.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture
statement: Vermicompost is prepared by using earthworms to decompose organic waste into nutrient-rich manure.

options.text: TRUE — earthworms produce nutrient-rich castings used as organic manure
FALSE — vermicompost is produced by burning organic waste at high temperature
FALSE — vermicompost is a synthetic chemical fertiliser from factories
FALSE — earthworms only damage crops and produce nothing useful

elimination_hints.hint: Burning produces ash, not vermicompost
Vermicompost is organic, not synthetic
Earthworms are beneficial, not damaging

question_text: Study the comparison table of common fertilisers used in India shown in the diagram and answer the question that follows.
explanation: The diagram compares seven common fertilisers by their NPK grade, type and key feature. DAP with grade 18-46-0 is a phosphatic complex supplying both nitrogen (18%) and phosphorus (46% P₂O₅) in one granule. Urea at 46-0-0 is the highest-N straight fertiliser. MOP at 0-0-60 is a potassic fertiliser. SSP supplies P plus sulphur together.
correct_answer_text: DAP with grade 18-46-0 supplies both nitrogen and phosphorus and is the most popular P source in India.
topic: Soil Science, Fertilizers, Manures and Biofertilisers
subject: agriculture

options.text: Urea with grade 18-46-0 is the most popular phosphatic fertiliser in India
DAP with grade 18-46-0 supplies nitrogen and phosphorus and is the top P source
MOP with grade 46-0-0 is a nitrogenous fertiliser used for top-dressing wheat
SSP with grade 0-0-60 is the principal potassic fertiliser used in India

elimination_hints.hint: Urea is 46-0-0, not 18-46-0; that is DAP
MOP is 0-0-60, not 46-0-0; that is urea
SSP is 0-16-0, not 0-0-60; that is MOP
